behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is in local wiring: directory discovery + filename parsing/sorting + execution + metadata recording. A unit test of sorting alone can miss real ordering bugs caused by file enumeration and runner orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Run the migration runner against a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., `002_b.sql`, `001_a.sql`, `010_c.sql`) and a local test Postgres DB. Each migration inserts its version into a test table; assert rows show execution order `1,2,10` and `_migrations` records the same ordered versions.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This depends on persisted state handoff between runs via `_migrations`. Pure in-process mocks can hide idempotency bugs (wrong lookup key, missing commit, bad filtering).
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice against the same local test Postgres DB and migration set. Include a migration with a visible side effect (e.g., insert a sentinel row). Assert `_migrations` contains one record per version and sentinel side effect occurs only once after both runs.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Rollback/stop behavior is at transaction + runner control-flow boundary. Mocking DB execution can falsely pass while real transaction behavior fails. No external network or LLM dependency is required.
behaviors.test_description: Use a local test Postgres DB with three migrations: first applies valid changes, second contains one valid statement then an intentional SQL error, third has a detectable side effect. Execute runner once; assert second migration’s partial changes are absent (rolled back), third migration was not applied, and `_migrations` marks only migrations completed before the failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary includes separate processes and OS-level concurrency timing. In-process mocks or single-process integration can miss races in locking/transactions that appear only under true concurrent runners.
behaviors.test_description: Start two independent runner processes simultaneously against the same local Postgres DB and identical migration directory. Use migrations that create unique, countable effects. After both processes exit, assert each migration effect exists exactly once and `_migrations` has exactly one row per version with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is local initialization sequencing between metadata bootstrap and migration execution. A unit test may miss ordering at the DB interaction boundary; no real external API/LLM is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh local test Postgres DB with no `_migrations` table, run migrations where the first migration expects `_migrations` to exist (e.g., references/reads it safely). Assert run succeeds, `_migrations` exists, and migration side effects are present. Optionally verify via DB logs/hooks that metadata table creation occurred before first migration execution.
